mn45:0.2: The Shorter Discourse on Undertakings of *dhamma*
mn45:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn45:2.3: There is, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is pleasant in the present and ripens in pain in the future;
mn45:2.4: there is, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is painful in the present and ripens in pain in the future;
mn45:2.5: there is, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is painful in the present and ripens in pleasure in the future;
mn45:2.6: there is, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is pleasant in the present and ripens in pleasure in the future.
mn45:3.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is pleasant in the present and ripens in pain in the future?
mn45:3.10: There they feel painful, intense, harsh, bitter *vedanās*.
mn45:3.12: ‘This is why those good ascetics and brahmins, seeing the future danger in sensual pleasures, spoke of abandoning sensual pleasures and prescribed the full understanding of sensual pleasures, for here we are, because of sensual pleasure, because sensual pleasure is the cause, feeling painful, intense, harsh, bitter *vedanās*.’
mn45:3.2: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view:
mn45:3.3: ‘There is no fault in sensual pleasures.’
mn45:3.5: They consort with female wanderers with topknots.
mn45:3.7: ‘Why is it that those good ascetics and brahmins, seeing the future danger in sensual pleasures, speak of abandoning sensual pleasures and prescribe the full understanding of sensual pleasures?
mn45:3.8: Pleasant is the touch of this female wanderer’s young, soft, downy arm.’ So they fall into sensual indulgence.
mn45:3.9: Having fallen into sensual indulgence, with the breakup of the body, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, a state of ruin, in hell.
mn45:4.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, in the last month of the hot season, a māluvā pod were to burst.
mn45:4.10: ‘Why was it that those good friends and companions, relatives and kin — park *devatās*, forest *devatās*, tree *devatās*, and *devatās* dwelling among herbs, grasses, and forest trees — seeing the future danger in the māluvā seed, having gathered together and assembled, reassured me thus:
mn45:4.11: “Do not be afraid, good one, do not be afraid.
mn45:4.12: Perhaps that māluvā seed will be swallowed by a peacock, or eaten by a deer, or burned by a forest fire, or removed by forest workers, or eaten by termites, or perhaps it will turn out seedless”?
mn45:4.13: Pleasant is the touch of this māluvā creeper, young, soft, downy, and dangling.’
mn45:4.14: It would keep encircling that sal tree.
mn45:4.15: Having encircled that sal tree, it would make a canopy above it.
mn45:4.16: Having made a canopy above it, it would produce a dense mass.
mn45:4.17: Having produced a dense mass, it would split apart the great boughs of that sal tree.
mn45:4.18: Then the *devatā* dwelling in that sal tree would think:
mn45:4.19: ‘This is why those good friends and companions, relatives and kin — park *devatās*, forest *devatās*, tree *devatās*, and *devatās* dwelling among herbs, grasses, and forest trees — seeing the future danger in the māluvā seed, having gathered together and assembled, reassured me thus:
mn45:4.2: Then that māluvā seed, *bhikkhus*, might fall at the root of a certain sal tree.
mn45:4.20: “Do not be afraid, good one, do not be afraid. Perhaps that māluvā seed will be swallowed by a peacock, or eaten by a deer, or burned by a forest fire, or removed by forest workers, or eaten by termites, or perhaps it will turn out seedless.”
mn45:4.21: And here I am, because of the māluvā seed, feeling painful, intense, harsh, bitter *vedanās*.’
mn45:4.22: Just so, *bhikkhus*, there are some ascetics and brahmins who hold this doctrine and view: ‘There is no fault in sensual pleasures.’
mn45:4.24: They consort with female wanderers with topknots.
mn45:4.26: ‘Why is it that those good ascetics and brahmins, seeing the future danger in sensual pleasures, speak of abandoning sensual pleasures and prescribe the full understanding of sensual pleasures?
mn45:4.27: Pleasant is the touch of this female wanderer’s young, soft, downy arm.’
mn45:4.29: Having fallen into sensual indulgence, with the breakup of the body, after death, they are reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, a state of ruin, in hell.
mn45:4.3: Then the *devatā* dwelling in that sal tree would become afraid, agitated, and fall into alarm.
mn45:4.30: There they feel painful, intense, harsh, bitter *vedanās*.
mn45:4.32: ‘This is why those good ascetics and brahmins, seeing the future danger in sensual pleasures, spoke of abandoning sensual pleasures and prescribed the full understanding of sensual pleasures.
mn45:4.33: For here we are, because of sensual pleasure, because sensual pleasure is the cause, feeling painful, intense, harsh, bitter *vedanās*.’
mn45:4.34: This is called, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is pleasant in the present and ripens in pain in the future.
mn45:4.4: Then the friends and companions, relatives and kin of the *devatā* dwelling in that sal tree — park *devatās*, forest *devatās*, tree *devatās*, and *devatās* dwelling among herbs, grasses, and forest trees — having gathered together and assembled, would reassure it thus:
mn45:4.5: ‘Do not be afraid, good one, do not be afraid.
mn45:4.6: Perhaps that māluvā seed will be swallowed by a peacock, or eaten by a deer, or burned by a forest fire, or removed by forest workers, or eaten by termites, or perhaps it will turn out seedless.’
mn45:4.7: But that māluvā seed, *bhikkhus*, would neither be swallowed by a peacock, nor eaten by a deer, nor burned by a forest fire, nor removed by forest workers, nor eaten by termites; and being thoroughly drenched by a rain cloud in the rainy season, it would sprout well.
mn45:4.8: That māluvā creeper, young, soft, downy, and dangling, would take hold of that sal tree.
mn45:4.9: Then the *devatā* dwelling in that sal tree would think:
mn45:5.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is painful in the present and ripens in pain in the future?
mn45:5.11: He squats, devoted to the practice of squatting.
mn45:5.12: He uses a bed of thorns and makes his bed on a bed of thorns.
mn45:5.13: He lives devoted to the practice of entering the water three times a day, including the evening.
mn45:5.14: Thus he lives devoted to the pursuit of tormenting and mortifying the body in many different ways.
mn45:5.15: With the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, a state of ruin, in hell.
mn45:5.16: This is called, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is painful in the present and ripens in pain in the future.
mn45:5.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone is a naked ascetic, going about freely, licking his hands, not coming when asked, not stopping when asked, not accepting food brought to him, food made especially for him, or an invitation.
mn45:5.3: He does not accept from the mouth of a pot, from the mouth of a bowl, across a threshold, across a stick, across a pestle, from two people eating, from a pregnant woman, from a nursing woman, from a woman in the midst of a man, from where food is announced, from where a dog is waiting, or from where flies are buzzing thickly; and he does not consume fish, meat, beer, wine, or rice-water.
mn45:5.4: He goes to one house and takes one mouthful, or to two houses and takes two mouthfuls … or to seven houses and takes seven mouthfuls.
mn45:5.5: He lives on one offering, or on two offerings … or on seven offerings.
mn45:5.6: He takes food once a day, or once every two days … or once every seven days. Thus he lives devoted to the practice of taking food at intervals, even for half a month.
mn45:5.7: He is an eater of greens, or millet, or wild rice, or scrapings, or chaff, or broken grain, or rice-scum, or oil-cake, or grass, or cow dung; he lives on forest roots and fruits, feeding on fallen fruit.
mn45:5.8: He wears hemp cloth, rough cloth, shrouds, rag-robes, bark-fiber garments, animal hide, strips of hide, kusa-grass garments, bark garments, wood-shaving garments, a human-hair blanket, a horsehair blanket, or a garment of owl feathers.
mn45:5.9: He plucks out hair and beard, devoted to the practice of plucking out hair and beard.
mn45:6.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is painful in the present and ripens in pleasure in the future?
mn45:6.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone is by nature of intensely lustful disposition; he frequently experiences pain and dejection born of lust.
mn45:6.3: He is by nature of intensely hateful disposition; he frequently experiences pain and dejection born of hate.
mn45:6.4: He is by nature of intensely deluded disposition; he frequently experiences pain and dejection born of delusion.
mn45:6.5: Though with pain, though with dejection, with tearful face and weeping, he lives the holy life complete and pure.
mn45:6.6: With the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn45:6.7: This is called, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is painful in the present and ripens in pleasure in the future.
mn45:7.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is pleasant in the present and ripens in pleasure in the future?
mn45:7.10: This is called, *bhikkhus*, an undertaking of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānaṁ*] that is pleasant in the present and ripens in pleasure in the future.
mn45:7.11: These, *bhikkhus*, are the four undertakings of *dhamma* [*dhammasamādānāni*].”
mn45:7.13: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn45:7.14: The Shorter Discourse on Undertakings of *dhamma* is finished, the fifth.
mn45:7.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone is by nature not of intensely lustful disposition; he does not frequently experience pain and dejection born of lust.
mn45:7.3: He is by nature not of intensely hateful disposition; he does not frequently experience pain and dejection born of hate.
mn45:7.4: He is by nature not of intensely deluded disposition; he does not frequently experience pain and dejection born of delusion.
mn45:7.5: Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn45:7.6: With the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*], with inner clarity and a unified state [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind [*cetaso*], he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which is without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicara*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* …
mn45:7.8: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn45:7.9: With the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
